title: Shadow-rival pairwise pressure model instead of full-field aggregates
summary: Recent F1 game-theoretic work argues that ignoring direct competition materially degrades pit strategy quality, and official analysis notes undercut/overcut decisions usually matter only when a rival is within striking range.
rationale: Recent F1 game-theoretic work argues that ignoring direct competition materially degrades pit strategy quality, and official analysis notes undercut/overcut decisions usually matter only when a rival is within striking range. That suggests a focused ‘most relevant opponent’ representation may outperform broad race-level summaries.
implementation_hint: Within each `Year+Race+LapNumber`, construct a shadow rival for each row: nearest car ahead/behind by position and same/adjacent stint phase; derive pairwise features like position proximity, tyre-age gap, degradation gap, lap-delta gap, compound mismatch, and whether pitting now likely jumps that rival after pit-loss priors. Train a specialist on these pairwise features and blend with the global model.
expected_effect: Could recover strategic interaction signal that simple position or race-level features miss, especially for midfield undercut battles where the next-lap decision is highly local.
risk: Without true time gaps, the shadow rival is approximate; bad rival matching can add structured noise.
